key_insight: The paper defines a “lattice gene” as a compact latent representation of local crystal structure from Kikuchi diffraction patterns, and a “lattice genome” as the spatial map of these genes across a microstructure to quantify heterogeneity.
engineering_relevance: This enables practical, data-driven characterization of mesoscale crystal variability in alloys, supporting microstructure design, process monitoring, and property control in manufacturing.
method: Encode EBSD Kikuchi patterns with a variational autoencoder to learn a low-dimensional lattice gene.
Build lattice genome maps by assigning latent vectors across representative areas.
Use latent-space distance, angle, and spread metrics for segmentation and heterogeneity quantification.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports validation on additively manufactured and wrought Ni-base superalloys in both as-built and recrystallized states, showing reconstruction ability and multiple analysis outputs.
watch_item: Performance depends on the quality and representativeness of EBSD diffraction data, and latent-space metrics may require careful calibration for different materials and scan conditions.
paper_title: Lattice genome: representation and analysis of heterogeneous crystalline microstructures
